literal: longing, desire, eager wish, appetite; action-noun from abhi-√laṣ 'to desire, to long toward' (abhi + laṣ + ghañ) — the directed reaching-wish for an object
invariant_definition: *Abhilāṣa* is directed longing — the appetite or eager wish that reaches *toward* (abhi-) an object (√laṣ, 'to desire'). It is a near-relative of *kāma* (desire), *tṛṣṇā* (thirst), and *spṛhā* (hankering), and the commentators on the *sthitaprajña* verses use it for the craving-wish that the freed one no longer has; but it is its own word — the leaning-toward of appetite — not identical to the standing thirst of *tṛṣṇā* or the formed desire of *kāma*.
translator_note: Render *abhilāṣa* as 'longing' or 'desire' — the directed appetite reaching toward an object (abhi-√laṣ). Keep it distinct from *tṛṣṇā* (thirst) and *spṛhā* (hankering): the commentators use these as allied but not identical words for wanting.
